title: --secret type=env,target= mapping lost after --restart=on-failure triggers
body:
### Issue Description

When a container is created with `--secret name,type=env,target=MY_TARGET` and `--restart=on-failure`, the `target=` mapping is lost after Podman restarts the container due to a failure. The environment variable key reverts to the literal secret name instead of the specified target.

On initial creation, `podman inspect` correctly shows `MY_TARGET=***`. After a crash-triggered restart, it shows `test-secret=***` instead.

This silently breaks any application that relies on specific environment variable names (e.g., `POSTGRES_PASSWORD`, `JWT_SECRET`), causing authentication failures after otherwise-recoverable crashes.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```bash
# Create a test secret
echo -n "secretvalue" | podman secret create test-secret -

# Run a container that will crash and be restarted by Podman
podman run -d \
  --name restart-target-test \
  --restart=on-failure:5 \
  --secret test-secret,type=env,target=MY_TARGET \
  docker.io/alpine:latest \
  sh -c 'sleep 2 && exit 1'

# Wait for at least one restart cycle
sleep 15

# Check the environment variable mapping
podman inspect restart-target-test \
  --format '{{range .Config.Env}}{{println .}}{{end}}' | grep -iE 'target|secret'

# Clean up
podman rm -f restart-target-test
podman secret rm test-secret
```

### Describe the results you received

After a crash-triggered restart, `podman inspect` shows:

```
test-secret=***
```

The `target=` mapping is lost and the literal secret name becomes the environment variable key.

### Describe the results you expected

After restart, `podman inspect` should show:

```
MY_TARGET=***
```

The `target=` mapping should be preserved across restarts, matching the behavior of the initial container creation.

### podman info output

```yaml
host:
  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.42.2
  cgroupControllers:
  - cpu
  - io
  - memory
  - pids
  cgroupManager: systemd
  cgroupVersion: v2
  conmon:
    package: conmon-2.1.13-1.fc42.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.1.13, commit: '
  cpus: 8
  databaseBackend: sqlite
  distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    variant: kde
    version: "42"
  eventLogger: journald
  hostname: fedora
  kernel: 6.18.5-100.fc42.x86_64
  linkmode: dynamic
  logDriver: journald
  memTotal: 33468936192
  networkBackend: netavark
  networkBackendInfo:
    backend: netavark
    dns:
      package: aardvark-dns-1.17.0-1.fc42.x86_64
      path: /usr/libexec/podman/aardvark-dns
      version: aardvark-dns 1.17.0
    package: netavark-1.17.1-1.fc42.x86_64
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/netavark
    version: netavark 1.17.1
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: crun-1.26-1.fc42.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version 1.26
      commit: 3241e671f92c33b0c003cd7de319e4f32add6231
      rundir: /run/user/1000/crun
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +LIBKRUN +WASM:wasmedge +YAJL
  os: linux
  rootlessNetworkCmd: pasta
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: false
    rootless: true
    seccompEnabled: true
    selinuxEnabled: true
  serviceIsRemote: false
store:
  configFile: /home/user/.config/containers/storage.conf
  containerStore:
    number: 24
    running: 24
  graphDriverName: overlay
  graphRoot: /home/user/.local/share/containers/storage
  graphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: btrfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "true"
    Supports d_type: "true"
  imageStore:
    number: 34
  transientStore: false
version:
  APIVersion: 5.7.1
  Built: 1765324800
  BuiltTime: Wed Dec 10 01:00:00 2025
  GitCommit: f845d14e941889ba4c071f35233d09b29d363c75
  GoVersion: go1.24.10
  OsArch: linux/amd64
  Version: 5.7.1
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes (5.7.1 is the latest available in Fedora 42 and 43)

### Additional environment details

Physical machine.

### Additional information

- A fresh `podman run` with the same `--secret` flags (without `--restart`) correctly applies `target=` every time. The issue only manifests when Podman itself restarts the container after a failure.
- The issue also reproduces inside pods (tested with `podman pod create` + `podman run --pod`).
- Real-world impact: services with multiple secrets mapped to specific env vars (e.g., `POSTGRES_PASSWORD`, `JWT_SECRET`, `DB_PASSWORD`) fail silently after a transient crash because the application can no longer find the expected variable names. The container appears "running" but the app inside is broken.
- Workaround: replace `--secret name,type=env,target=VAR` with `-e VAR=value` at the cost of losing secret masking in `podman inspect`.

comments.body:
@tluj i can see the issue, but i also state that while i was reproducing this, i can see that podman exec is returning the true and real env variables, the problem lies in the podman inspect,

```zsh
amol@Amols-MBP ~ % podman run -d \
  --name bug-repro \
  --restart=on-failure:5 \
  --secret test-secret,type=env,target=REAL_ENV_NAME \
  alpine sh -c 'sleep 60 && exit 1'
a61681ac787dedf38e36f1b2aae6367c200cb83573fe2c278642d5c75e54c90a
amol@Amols-MBP ~ % podman exec bug-repro env | grep -iE 'REAL_ENV_NAME|test-secret'
REAL_ENV_NAME=my-super-secret-value
```

@amyssnippet Thanks for looking at this. However, I think your test checked the container before it crashed and was restarted. The target= mapping works correctly on the initial run. The issue is that it's lost after --restart=on-failure triggers a restart.
To reproduce fully, you need to wait for the container to exit and be restarted by Podman:
```bash
podman run -d \
  --name bug-repro \
  --restart=on-failure:5 \
  --secret test-secret,type=env,target=REAL_ENV_NAME \
  alpine sh -c 'sleep 2 && exit 1'

sleep 15

podman exec bug-repro env | grep -iE 'REAL_ENV_NAME|test-secret'
```
After the restart cycle, I expect podman exec will also show test-secret=... instead of REAL_ENV_NAME=..., since the application-level failures I'm seeing (database auth failures after crash recovery) confirm the actual env var name changes, not just the inspect output.
i see, but that just a workaround, i guess this maybe fixed in #28076

@amyssnippet appreciate the fast PR. Just want to make sure we're on the same page though. The main problem isn't how inspect displays the variable. The actual env var inside the container changes after a restart. My apps break because something like POSTGRES_PASSWORD stops existing and gets replaced with the literal secret name. Your PR description mentions it's a display only fix that doesn't affect runtime behavior, so I think the restart logic bug still needs its own fix. Can this issue stay open after #28076 merges?

@tluj after reading to the issue i think this is just a problem in the inspect subcommand, regarding how it is showing the env vars. and thats what i fixed there as a display only fix, so lemme repro this with my examples, and will get back on the pr and this issue soon.


this issue will be solved in the #28076 anyhow

@amyssnippet Sounds good, let me know what you find. When you repro, the key thing is to check podman exec after the container has crashed and been restarted by podman, not on the first run. Use sleep 2 && exit 1 so it cycles fast, wait about 15 seconds, then run podman exec bug-repro env | grep -iE 'REAL_ENV_NAME|test-secret'. If the actual env var inside the container still shows REAL_ENV_NAME after a restart then I'll happily stand corrected. But my services were failing with real database auth errors after crash recoveries, which points to more than just a display issue.
@tluj ig now the pr will solve your issue. and by merging the pr will close this issue automatically
@Honny1 i am solving it, can you assign me this
@tluj this might work, just update your podman binary
If we do a 5.8.1 (still not sure if/when we will) the fix can be included. Otherwise, 6.0 in May will definitely have the fix.
@mheon he can clone the latest code and build himself right?? then it can work, just update the system path
Yes, building latest from source would be a way to test.
